facts.text: ADHD has a heritability rate of 70–80%, making genetics its most significant causal factor.
The remaining 20–30% of ADHD variance is mediated by de-novo mutations and non-shared environmental factors, not family environment.
DSM-5 requires at least six symptoms of inattention or hyperactivity/impulsivity for those under 17, but only five for those 17 or older.
ADHD symptoms must be present in at least two settings (e.g., school, home, work) to meet DSM-5 diagnostic criteria.
Several ADHD symptoms must have been present before age 12 according to DSM-5 criteria.
ADHD is classified as a neurodevelopmental disorder, not purely a behavioral or psychiatric one.
Hyperfocus in ADHD is caused by inhibitory deficits — difficulty stopping an already ongoing response — rather than by heightened concentration ability.
Hyperfocus is associated with risks such as addiction and certain types of offending.
ADHD symptoms arise specifically from executive dysfunction, not general cognitive impairment.
ADHD involves imbalances in both the noradrenergic and dopaminergic neurotransmitter systems.
The dopamine and norepinephrine pathways implicated in ADHD originate in the ventral tegmental area and locus coeruleus.
These pathways project to the prefrontal cortex and striatum, governing executive function, motivation, reward perception, and motor function.
ADHD can very rarely result from chromosomal abnormalities.
Most ADHD diagnoses in children are initiated after a teacher raises concerns, not parents.
ADHD diagnosis in children is more common in low-income families and in Caucasian children compared to Black, Hispanic, and Asian children.
There is no biological test for ADHD; diagnosis is based on behavioral and mental development assessment.
The International Consensus Statement on ADHD concluded that the lack of a biological test does not invalidate the diagnosis.
Medications used to treat ADHD include stimulants, atomoxetine, alpha-2 adrenergic receptor agonists, and sometimes antidepressants.
Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant medication approved for ADHD treatment.
ADHD medications can reduce some elevated risks such as obesity.
Large amounts of positive reinforcement improve task performance in individuals with ADHD who struggle to focus on long-term rewards.
ADHD is associated with reduced size, functional connectivity, and activation in prefrontal executive networks.
ADHD impairments include poor time management, cognitive inhibition deficits, difficulty with task initiation, and poor sustained attention.
ADHD is associated with a reduction in life expectancy due to cumulative health risks.
Symptoms of ADHD must persist for six months or more and be developmentally inappropriate relative to peers for a DSM-5 diagnosis.
In adults, hyperactivity in ADHD typically manifests as restlessness rather than overt physical overactivity.
The rearing family and social environment have no significant contribution to ADHD causation according to genetic research.
ADHD diagnosis may be influenced by racial and socioeconomic biases, contributing to disparities in who receives a diagnosis.
ADHD involves perseveration — continuing actions despite intending to stop — due to difficulty interrupting ongoing response patterns.
ADHD management typically involves counseling, medications, or a combination of both.
